assignments.title: Review: HTML Debugging
assignments.description_html:
Fork this CodePen to your account: https://codepen.io/team/anniecannons/pen/jOjmOPz 

The above HTML CodePen contains many errors.

Your job is to debug the HTML and fix the errors.

Suggested approach:

  • Look at the HTML and fix all of the errors you see immediately.
  • Use "Analyze HTML" in CodePen to help you identify errors you've missed.
  • Rather than trying to fix every error at once, fix one error, then re-run Analyze HTML. You will find that some "errors" are not truly there. This is because a previous error made the software think another error was present.

In general, look for these kinds of errors:

  • unclosed tags
  • poor syntax (the punctuation that makes up HTML)
  • bad nesting of HTML elements (remember that the tags close in the opposite order in which they are opened)
  • The wrong element used for the situation

assignments.title: Grid 1 Exercise
assignments.description_html:
Fork and save this CodePen to your account: https://codepen.io/team/anniecannons/pen/OJegQVG 

Add CSS to your page so that:

  • each grid item (child) has a border and a background color
  • create a grid so that the items goes across the page in a row
  • change the order of the grid items on tablet and mobile as described below
    • On desktop, the order should be 1, 2, 3

Write media queries so that there are

  • two columns for tablet
    • have the order to the grid items go 3, 2, 1
  •  one column for mobile
    • have the order to the grid items go 1, 2, 3

BONUS

assignments.title: Weather App React Copy
assignments.description_html:
Let's practice our skills!

We will create a weather app that asks the user for a city and then shows the current weather back to the user after fetching data from an API.

  • Your instructor will provide an API key
  • Use this URL for the API call: 
    https://api.openweathermap.org/data/2.5/weather?q=${city}&units=imperial&appid=${apiKey}
    • Make sure to create a variable called apiKey that stores your key
  • The weather card should be a component
  • App.jsx will be your home page
  • Which file should the form be in?
  • Which file should the API call be in?

assignments.title: To-Do List + Focus Friday Plan
assignments.description_html:
Last term, you used Thursday afternoons to write weekly blogs. This term, we’re shifting that time toward building a different,  but equally important, skill: tracking your tasks and planning your time.

Being able to organize what you’ve completed, what still needs to be done, and how you plan to use your independent time is essential to becoming a reliable, collaborative, and effective developer. On real teams, people depend on each other to:

  • Know what work is in progress
  • Communicate when things are stuck
  • Plan time effectively
  • Follow through and share outcomes

That’s exactly what this new routine is designed to help you practice.

Every Thursday, you’ll update your To-Do List and write out a clear Focus Friday Plan.

Please use the Daily Work Journal template to submit, this is the template you are filling out each day:

  • Notion TemplateLinks to an external site.
  • Google Doc TemplateLinks to an external site.

Then on Monday morning, you’ll take one minute to share what you were able to accomplish. It’s not about doing everything perfectly — it’s about building strong habits and learning how to communicate like a pro.

Submit the link here. Be sure that your instructor(s) can access your document!

assignments.title: Tic-Tac-Toe game layout with click
assignments.description_html:
Starting Codepen: https://codepen.io/team/anniecannons/pen/myyNNGN

  1. Make sure to leave comments in your code
  2. Create the following layout (Image description below)
    1. Write your code in Codepen
    2. Use CSS Grid
    3. Use any colors you like
  3. Save each card into JavaScript variables
    1. You will need to add a second class to each of the cards. Card one already has a second class added as an example.
  4. Add code inside of the pre-made functions, then attach event listeners to make each pink square clickable as follows
    1. When the user clicks, it should say, "Square one was clicked!" in the console
    2. When the user clicks, turn the background color white
      1. Hint: Add a new class in CSS with a background color of white
    3. When the user clicks, change the number 3 to the word 'three'
    4. Inside the function for square 4, declare a variable called "message" and set it to a string that says "This is the secret message for the 4th square."
      1. Still inside the function, log the variable to the console
    5. In the CSS, look at the style rule for .hidden ~ what does display: none do? Feel free to Google
      1. When the user clicks the 5th square, it should disappear from the screen
      2. Add the class of .hidden when the user clicks the button (the class is already added to the CSS)
    6. Write your own function that will add a border to the 6th square when the user clicks it
    7. Write your own function to change the text of square one when square 7 is clicked. Change the text to anything you want
    8. Write your own function to change the color of the body's background when square 8 is clicked. 
    9. Write a function so that all of the cards disappear when clicked. 
      1. HINT: Look at the HTML. What is the parent of the cards? If you make the parent disappear, all of the cards will too!

Image description:

The image shows a 3x3 grid of numbered buttons centered on a dark blue background. Each button is a pink square with bold black numbers in the center. The grid is surrounded by a thick white border, giving it the appearance of a keypad or game board. The numbers are arranged from 1 to 9 in chronological order.

Instructions:

  • Create a new CodePen for your design.
  • Write HTML that would achieve the above layout.
  • Write CSS to achieve this layout.

assignments.title: To-Do List + Focus Friday Plan
assignments.description_html:
Last term, you used Thursday afternoons to write weekly blogs. This term, we’re shifting that time toward building a different,  but equally important, skill: tracking your tasks and planning your time.

Being able to organize what you’ve completed, what still needs to be done, and how you plan to use your independent time is essential to becoming a reliable, collaborative, and effective developer. On real teams, people depend on each other to:

  • Know what work is in progress
  • Communicate when things are stuck
  • Plan time effectively
  • Follow through and share outcomes

That’s exactly what this new routine is designed to help you practice.

Every Thursday, you’ll update your To-Do List and write out a clear Focus Friday Plan.

Please use the Daily Work Journal template to submit, this is the template you are filling out each day:

  • Notion Template
  • Google Doc Template

Then on Monday morning, you’ll take one minute to share what you were able to accomplish. It’s not about doing everything perfectly — it’s about building strong habits and learning how to communicate like a pro.

assignments.title: Mood Matcher: combining forms, dot/bracket, if/else, DOM update
assignments.description_html:
Overview

You will build a simple app where a user selects their mood from a dropdown, enters a quick description, and the app returns a custom message and image based on the mood. This project will involve a form, submit, dot/bracket notation, if/else, and DOM updates.

See the site here: https://codepen.io/team/anniecannons/live/EaaELea/b520de0b0ad382d9a4efaa2bf7edc7e1

✅ Requirements

  1. Build in codepen or VSCode

  2. Form Inputs:

    • Dropdown for mood (e.g., Happy, Sad, Calm)

    • Text input for a brief note (“Why do you feel this way?”)

    • Submit button

  3. User Results

    • Under your form in the HTML, create a section called “result.”

      • Inside that section, add:

        • a heading tag for the message (leave it blank),

        • a paragraph for the user’s note (leave it blank),

        • an image tag with no src or alt yet.

        • These will look empty at first — that’s okay! In the JavaScript you will later fill them in. You are not creating these elements in JS, only updating them.

  4. JavaScript Functionality:

    • Create three variables for the HTML elements that you created for the User Results section.
    • Create three variables at the top of your file: one for output, one for imgAlt, and one for imgSrc

        • These will start as empty values and will be reassigned inside your if/else if statements.

    • Use boilerplate code to create the object from your form

    • After you see the data in the console and it looks right, you can add more lines of code to your submit function to finish the project

      • Save the data from the form into variables using dot notation. One for mood and one for note.

    • Use if/else else/if statements to match the user's mood to a message/image

      • Reassign the output, imgAlt and imgSrc variables inside of the if/else statements

    • When the user clicks submit, update the DOM with:

      • A message based on the mood

      • The note the user submitted

      • A related emoji or image

  5. Image src links
    1. Happy: https://cdn-icons-png.flaticon.com/512/742/742751.png
    2. Sad: https://cdn-icons-png.flaticon.com/512/742/742752.png
    3. Calm: https://cdn-icons-png.flaticon.com/512/5786/5786952.png

  6. Bonus (Optional):

    • Use .classList to change background color or add mood-specific styles

    • Include a reset button that clears the displayed result

assignments.title: Week 3 Explain Your Code
assignments.description_html:
Overview

Each developer will choose one to present from the list below:

  • Mood Matcher
  • Any loops assignments
  • Story Conditionals: Finances

Please only choose 5 lines of code to present. 

You will share your Codepen link in the Zoom link and let us know which lines of code to look at.

How to prepare for your presentation

  1. Make sure you understand the code you submit throughout this course

    • It's often easy to find code that works — but that’s not enough.
    • Our goal is for you to take the time to learn why the code works and how to write it yourself before turning it in.

  2.  Clean up your code

    • Rename any confusing variables or functions.
    • Remove unused code.
    • Organize your logic into clear, readable sections.
    • Aim for readability — your code should feel like reading a well-organized newspaper: easy to scan, logical, and understandable at a glance.

  3. Add helpful comments

    • Write comments to remind yourself what each section of the code is doing and why.
    • This not only helps you during your presentation, but also for future review. 

Why this matters

This exercise is about talking through your code. It will help you:

  • Deepen your understanding
  • Improve how you communicate as a developer
  • Build your confidence when sharing work with others

You don’t have to be perfect. Mistakes and stumbles are part of learning! We’ll support each other and give helpful feedback. The more you practice, the stronger your skills will become.

Presentation Logistics

  1. The instructor will randomly assign a presentation order (with room for preferences).
  2. Before presenting, take a few minutes to:
    • Hide private content (e.g., Slack DMs).
    • Close any unrelated tabs or programs.
    • Take a deep breath, grab some water or a tactile toy — whatever helps you feel grounded and confident.
    • Open your project in VS Code and the browser.
  3. Presentations will follow the finalized order.
  4. After each presentation:
    • We’ll have a brief Q&A.
    • The instructor may give feedback or ask a technical question.
    • Students are welcome to ask questions or share thoughts, too.

Make-up option if you miss class

If you miss class, you may present your code to the instructor one-on-one during lab hours to receive credit.

How to turn this in

There’s nothing to submit. Once you present your code in class, your instructor will mark this assignment as complete.

assignments.title: Week 4 Explain Your Code
assignments.description_html:
Overview

Each developer will be called upon to present a few lines of code from the following codepen:

  • Game Scoring System Quiz Code

How to prepare for your presentation

  1. Make sure you understand the code you submit throughout this course

    • It's often easy to find code that works — but that’s not enough.
    • Our goal is for you to take the time to learn why the code works and how to write it yourself before turning it in.

  2.  Clean up your code

    • Rename any confusing variables or functions.
    • Remove unused code.
    • Organize your logic into clear, readable sections.
    • Aim for readability — your code should feel like reading a well-organized newspaper: easy to scan, logical, and understandable at a glance.

  3. Add helpful comments

    • Write comments to remind yourself what each section of the code is doing and why.
    • This not only helps you during your presentation, but also for future review. 

Why this matters

This exercise is about talking through your code. It will help you:

  • Deepen your understanding
  • Improve how you communicate as a developer
  • Build your confidence when sharing work with others

You don’t have to be perfect. Mistakes and stumbles are part of learning! We’ll support each other and give helpful feedback. The more you practice, the stronger your skills will become.

Presentation Logistics

  1. The instructor will randomly assign a presentation order (with room for preferences).
  2. Before presenting, take a few minutes to:
    • Hide private content (e.g., Slack DMs).
    • Close any unrelated tabs or programs.
    • Take a deep breath, grab some water or a tactile toy — whatever helps you feel grounded and confident.
    • Open your project in VS Code and the browser.
  3. Presentations will follow the finalized order.
  4. After each presentation:
    • We’ll have a brief Q&A.
    • The instructor may give feedback or ask a technical question.
    • Students are welcome to ask questions or share thoughts, too.

Make-up option if you miss class

If you miss class, you may present your code to the instructor one-on-one during lab hours to receive credit.

How to turn this in

There’s nothing to submit. Once you present your code in class, your instructor will mark this assignment as complete.

assignments.title: Week 14: To-Do List + Focus Friday Plan
assignments.description_html:
Last term, you used Thursday afternoons to write weekly blogs. This term, we’re shifting that time toward building a different,  but equally important, skill: tracking your tasks and planning your time.

Being able to organize what you’ve completed, what still needs to be done, and how you plan to use your independent time is essential to becoming a reliable, collaborative, and effective developer. On real teams, people depend on each other to:

  • Know what work is in progress
  • Communicate when things are stuck
  • Plan time effectively
  • Follow through and share outcomes

That’s exactly what this new routine is designed to help you practice.

Every Thursday, you’ll update your To-Do List and write out a clear Focus Friday Plan. Then on Monday morning, you’ll take one minute to share what you were able to accomplish. It’s not about doing everything perfectly — it’s about building strong habits and learning how to communicate like a pro.

How to turn this in

Submit the link here. Be sure that your instructor(s) can access your document!

 

Late assignments/ Incomplete Assignments

In the case of incomplete assignments, please note that it is crucial to provide as much information as possible. This will help us understand why you were unable to meet the deadline and how we can best support you in completing the work.

In the comments for the assignment, please include the following:

  1. Why the assignment is late
  2. What you still need to do for the assignment
  3. Your expected timeline for completing the work
  4. If you would like to copy and paste the example here as a template please do so

**You must include a link in order for your submission to work. Feel free to use www.google.com.

For example:

 “The ________ project is not complete because _________. I have ________, ________, and ________ complete but still need more time to complete _________, _________, and _________. I estimate that I will have this done by _________________.”

assignments.title: Week 6: To-Do List + Focus Friday Plan
assignments.description_html:
Last term, you used Thursday afternoons to write weekly blogs. This term, we’re shifting that time toward building a different,  but equally important, skill: tracking your tasks and planning your time.

Being able to organize what you’ve completed, what still needs to be done, and how you plan to use your independent time is essential to becoming a reliable, collaborative, and effective developer. On real teams, people depend on each other to:

  • Know what work is in progress
  • Communicate when things are stuck
  • Plan time effectively
  • Follow through and share outcomes

That’s exactly what this new routine is designed to help you practice.

Every Thursday, you’ll update your To-Do List and write out a clear Focus Friday Plan.

Please use the Daily Work Journal template to submit, this is the template you are filling out each day:

  • Notion Template
  • Google Doc Template

 

 Then on Monday morning, you’ll take one minute to share what you were able to accomplish. It’s not about doing everything perfectly — it’s about building strong habits and learning how to communicate like a pro.

assignments.title: Prompt Engineering Course
assignments.description_html:
Prompt Engineering Course Overview

🧠 Grow with Google—Coursera Course Series

You’ve been invited to join the Grow with Google Learning Program on Coursera—an opportunity to build in-demand skills with Generative AI.

🔑 Getting Started

  1. Check your inbox for the invite to the program.
  2. Accept the invite and notify Dawn once completed.
  3. You will then be added to the course titled
     Generative AI Introduction and Prompting in Coursera on Coursera.

📘 Course Requirements
You’ll see four courses in the series.

  • ✅ Required: Google Prompting Essentials
  • 📌 Optional: The remaining three courses can be completed independently, outside of class.

Estimated time to complete: ~6 hours
Deadline: Complete the required course by Wednesday, June 25, 2025

🎯 Why This Course Matters
As developers, clear and effective use of AI tools is now a core skill. Whether you're debugging, planning content, or analyzing data, knowing how to write strong prompts helps you:

  • Save time
  • Improve accuracy
  • Get better results from AI tools

The course uses Google’s Generative AI, but feel free to apply what you learn using ChatGPT, Claude, Gemini, Perplexity, or any other tool of your choice.

📚 What You’ll Learn

  • How to structure prompts using the Task → Context → Evaluate → Iterate framework
  • Techniques for brainstorming, summarizing, rewriting, and formatting
  • Prompting for data analysis, visualizations, and spreadsheet fixes
  • Advanced skills like prompt chaining and prompt versioning

✅ What You’ll Walk Away With

  • A strong foundational framework for prompt writing
  • Tools for working faster and smarter with AI
  • Enhanced communication skills for technical and non-technical tasks
  • A resume-ready skill: Prompt Engineering
  • Confidence using AI in real-world dev tasks

🌟 New: Expanded Coursera Access By accepting your invite, you now have access to additional courses and career certificates within the Grow with Google Coursera program. If you find a course or pathway that interests you, book time with Dawn to talk about how it could align with your career goals.

📤 Submission Requirements
To receive credit for completing the Prompt Engineering course:

  1. Upload a screenshot of your course completion (from Coursera).

  2. Complete the Post-Course Survey here.

Need help or haven’t received your course invite?
 📩 Message Dawn directly via Slack.
 💬 Questions about the course or your progress? Reach out any time!

assignments.title: Week 8: To-Do List + Focus Friday Plan
assignments.description_html:
Last term, you used Thursday afternoons to write weekly blogs. This term, we’re shifting that time toward building a different,  but equally important, skill: tracking your tasks and planning your time.

Being able to organize what you’ve completed, what still needs to be done, and how you plan to use your independent time is essential to becoming a reliable, collaborative, and effective developer. On real teams, people depend on each other to:

  • Know what work is in progress
  • Communicate when things are stuck
  • Plan time effectively
  • Follow through and share outcomes

That’s exactly what this new routine is designed to help you practice.

Every Thursday, you’ll update your To-Do List and write out a clear Focus Friday Plan.

Please use the Daily Work Journal template to submit, this is the template you are filling out each day:

  • Notion TemplateLinks to an external site.
  • Google Doc TemplateLinks to an external site.

Then on Monday morning, you’ll take one minute to share what you were able to accomplish. It’s not about doing everything perfectly — it’s about building strong habits and learning how to communicate like a pro.

assignments.title: Week 9 Explain Your Code
assignments.description_html:
Overview

This week we will attempt to read through a piece of code we have never seen. This will be related to the React code read along assignment. 

StackBlitz

How to prepare for your presentation

  1. Make sure you understand the code you submit throughout this course

    • It's often easy to find code that works — but that’s not enough.
    • Our goal is for you to take the time to learn why the code works and how to write it yourself before turning it in.

  2.  Clean up your code

    • Rename any confusing variables or functions.
    • Remove unused code.
    • Organize your logic into clear, readable sections.
    • Aim for readability — your code should feel like reading a well-organized newspaper: easy to scan, logical, and understandable at a glance.

  3. Add helpful comments

    • Write comments to remind yourself what each section of the code is doing and why.
    • This not only helps you during your presentation, but also for future review. 

Why this matters

This exercise is about talking through your code. It will help you:

  • Deepen your understanding
  • Improve how you communicate as a developer
  • Build your confidence when sharing work with others

You don’t have to be perfect. Mistakes and stumbles are part of learning! We’ll support each other and give helpful feedback. The more you practice, the stronger your skills will become.

Presentation Logistics

  1. The instructor will randomly assign a presentation order (with room for preferences).
  2. Before presenting, take a few minutes to:
    • Hide private content (e.g., Slack DMs).
    • Close any unrelated tabs or programs.
    • Take a deep breath, grab some water or a tactile toy — whatever helps you feel grounded and confident.
    • Open your project in VS Code and the browser.
  3. Presentations will follow the finalized order.
  4. After each presentation:
    • We’ll have a brief Q&A.
    • The instructor may give feedback or ask a technical question.
    • Students are welcome to ask questions or share thoughts, too.

Make-up option if you miss class

If you miss class, you may present your code to the instructor one-on-one during lab hours to receive credit.

How to turn this in

There’s nothing to submit. Once you present your code in class, your instructor will mark this assignment as complete.

assignments.title: Week 9: To-Do List + Focus Friday Plan
assignments.description_html:
Last term, you used Thursday afternoons to write weekly blogs. This term, we’re shifting that time toward building a different,  but equally important, skill: tracking your tasks and planning your time.

Being able to organize what you’ve completed, what still needs to be done, and how you plan to use your independent time is essential to becoming a reliable, collaborative, and effective developer. On real teams, people depend on each other to:

  • Know what work is in progress
  • Communicate when things are stuck
  • Plan time effectively
  • Follow through and share outcomes

That’s exactly what this new routine is designed to help you practice.

Every Thursday, you’ll update your To-Do List and write out a clear Focus Friday Plan.

Please use the Daily Work Journal template to submit, this is the template you are filling out each day:

  • Notion TemplateLinks to an external site.
  • Google Doc TemplateLinks to an external site.

Then on Monday morning, you’ll take one minute to share what you were able to accomplish. It’s not about doing everything perfectly — it’s about building strong habits and learning how to communicate like a pro.

assignments.title: Ternary Statement Practice
assignments.description_html:
You can use this Stackblitz for this assignment: https://stackblitz.com/edit/vitejs-vite-wga8mqc8?file=src%2FApp.jsx

  1. Create a component called ProductCard.

  2. Add a useState variable called inStock, defaulted to true.

  3. Show a product name (you can hardcode something like "Vintage Backpack").

  4. Conditionally render:

    • A message below the name:

      • "Available now!" if inStock is true

      • "Sorry, we're out of stock." if inStock is false

    • A button with text:

      • "Mark as Out of Stock" if inStock is true

      • "Restock Item" if inStock is false

  5. When the button is clicked, toggle the value of inStock. (If the current value is true, make it false and vice versa)
    If the value of inStock is false, the card should look like this:

  6. If the value of inStock is true, the card should look like this:
  7. Clicking the button should toggle the value inStock of from true to false and vice versa
  8. In App.jsx, import and render the product card as many times as you want

BONUS: Map over data and create a card for each item in the array

  1. In App.jsx, import the data from  data.js:
    import data from './data.js';
  2. Change productCard so that it accepts a prop called data
  3. In App.jsx, in the JSX, map over the array called data (you imported this at the top and can now use data as a variable in your file).
  4. For each item in the array, render a ProductCard, and pass the inStock value as the props:
    <ProductCard data={item.inStock} />
  5. In the productCard, update where you have "Vintage Card" to {name}

assignments.title: Week 11: To-Do List + Focus Friday Plan
assignments.description_html:
Last term, you used Thursday afternoons to write weekly blogs. This term, we’re shifting that time toward building a different,  but equally important, skill: tracking your tasks and planning your time.

Being able to organize what you’ve completed, what still needs to be done, and how you plan to use your independent time is essential to becoming a reliable, collaborative, and effective developer. On real teams, people depend on each other to:

  • Know what work is in progress
  • Communicate when things are stuck
  • Plan time effectively
  • Follow through and share outcomes

That’s exactly what this new routine is designed to help you practice.

Every Thursday, you’ll update your To-Do List and write out a clear Focus Friday Plan.

Please use the Daily Work Journal template to submit, this is the template you are filling out each day:

  • Notion TemplateLinks to an external site.
  • Google Doc TemplateLinks to an external site.

Then on Monday morning, you’ll take one minute to share what you were able to accomplish. It’s not about doing everything perfectly — it’s about building strong habits and learning how to communicate like a pro.

assignments.title: Week 11 Explain Your Code
assignments.description_html:
Overview

This week we will review the Weather App API

How to prepare for your presentation

  1. Make sure you understand the code you submit throughout this course

    • It's often easy to find code that works — but that’s not enough.
    • Our goal is for you to take the time to learn why the code works and how to write it yourself before turning it in.

  2.  Clean up your code

    • Rename any confusing variables or functions.
    • Remove unused code.
    • Organize your logic into clear, readable sections.
    • Aim for readability — your code should feel like reading a well-organized newspaper: easy to scan, logical, and understandable at a glance.

  3. Add helpful comments

    • Write comments to remind yourself what each section of the code is doing and why.
    • This not only helps you during your presentation, but also for future review. 

Why this matters

This exercise is about talking through your code. It will help you:

  • Deepen your understanding
  • Improve how you communicate as a developer
  • Build your confidence when sharing work with others

You don’t have to be perfect. Mistakes and stumbles are part of learning! We’ll support each other and give helpful feedback. The more you practice, the stronger your skills will become.

Presentation Logistics

  1. The instructor will randomly assign a presentation order (with room for preferences).
  2. Before presenting, take a few minutes to:
    • Hide private content (e.g., Slack DMs).
    • Close any unrelated tabs or programs.
    • Take a deep breath, grab some water or a tactile toy — whatever helps you feel grounded and confident.
    • Open your project in VS Code and the browser.
  3. Presentations will follow the finalized order.
  4. After each presentation:
    • We’ll have a brief Q&A.
    • The instructor may give feedback or ask a technical question.
    • Students are welcome to ask questions or share thoughts, too.

Make-up option if you miss class

If you miss class, you may present your code to the instructor one-on-one during lab hours to receive credit.

How to turn this in

There’s nothing to submit. Once you present your code in class, your instructor will mark this assignment as complete.

assignments.title: Week 11 Clean & Comment Your Code
assignments.description_html:
Overview

  • During Block A on Thursdays, you’ll review, clean, and organize all of the code you’ve written that week. We will ask you to turn in one thing.
  • You’ll add helpful comments that explain what your code does and why
  • Refer to this guide: Cleaning & Commenting Your Code Best Practices.

Why this matters

  • It helps you understand your own logic more deeply
  • Clean, readable code is easier to debug and maintain
  • Commenting your code is a professional standard on dev teams
  • Good documentation supports your peers and your future self

How to turn this in

  • Please submit the Props Practice Exercise. Your instructor will review your code and comments in this Codepen.

assignments.title: Week 13: To-Do List + Focus Friday Plan
assignments.description_html:
Last term, you used Thursday afternoons to write weekly blogs. This term, we’re shifting that time toward building a different,  but equally important, skill: tracking your tasks and planning your time.

Being able to organize what you’ve completed, what still needs to be done, and how you plan to use your independent time is essential to becoming a reliable, collaborative, and effective developer. On real teams, people depend on each other to:

  • Know what work is in progress
  • Communicate when things are stuck
  • Plan time effectively
  • Follow through and share outcomes

That’s exactly what this new routine is designed to help you practice.

Every Thursday, you’ll update your To-Do List and write out a clear Focus Friday Plan.

Please use the Daily Work Journal template to submit, this is the template you are filling out each day:

  • Notion TemplateLinks to an external site.
  • Google Doc TemplateLinks to an external site.

Then on Monday morning, you’ll take one minute to share what you were able to accomplish. It’s not about doing everything perfectly — it’s about building strong habits and learning how to communicate like a pro.

Submit the link here. Be sure that your instructor(s) can access your document!

assignments.title: Week 13 Explain Your Code
assignments.description_html:
Overview

 

How to prepare for your presentation

  1. Make sure you understand the code you submit throughout this course

    • It's often easy to find code that works — but that’s not enough.
    • Our goal is for you to take the time to learn why the code works and how to write it yourself before turning it in.

  2.  Clean up your code

    • Rename any confusing variables or functions.
    • Remove unused code.
    • Organize your logic into clear, readable sections.
    • Aim for readability — your code should feel like reading a well-organized newspaper: easy to scan, logical, and understandable at a glance.

  3. Add helpful comments

    • Write comments to remind yourself what each section of the code is doing and why.
    • This not only helps you during your presentation, but also for future review. 

Why this matters

This exercise is about talking through your code. It will help you:

  • Deepen your understanding
  • Improve how you communicate as a developer
  • Build your confidence when sharing work with others

You don’t have to be perfect. Mistakes and stumbles are part of learning! We’ll support each other and give helpful feedback. The more you practice, the stronger your skills will become.

Presentation Logistics

  1. The instructor will randomly assign a presentation order (with room for preferences).
  2. Before presenting, take a few minutes to:
    • Hide private content (e.g., Slack DMs).
    • Close any unrelated tabs or programs.
    • Take a deep breath, grab some water or a tactile toy — whatever helps you feel grounded and confident.
    • Open your project in VS Code and the browser.
  3. Presentations will follow the finalized order.
  4. After each presentation:
    • We’ll have a brief Q&A.
    • The instructor may give feedback or ask a technical question.
    • Students are welcome to ask questions or share thoughts, too.

Make-up option if you miss class

If you miss class, you may present your code to the instructor one-on-one during lab hours to receive credit.

How to turn this in

There’s nothing to submit. Once you present your code in class, your instructor will mark this assignment as complete.

assignments.title: 📄 Assignment: Huntr Weekly Job Search Tracking Goal
assignments.description_html:
Instructions

As part of your ongoing job search readiness, you are required to actively track your job search progress in Huntr each week. This assignment will be tracked through Huntr and marked complete by the AnnieCannons (AC) Career Development team.

Weekly Requirement:

✅ Save at least 5 relevant job opportunities to your Huntr board.
✅ BONUS- Apply to at least 1 job from your saved list.

Submission:

You do not need to submit anything directly here.
The AC team will review your Huntr board weekly to verify that you’ve met the requirement.

Notes:

  • Make sure your Huntr board is kept up to date and clearly labeled with the jobs you’ve saved and applied for.

  • If you need support identifying jobs to save or crafting applications, reach out during office hours or post your question in Slack.

Grading:

This assignment is complete/incomplete, based on the following criteria:

  • At least 5 jobs saved on Huntr each week.

assignments.description_html:
Age Calculator App (Junior level)

Your challenge is to build out this age calculator app and get it looking as close to the design as possible.

You can use any tools you like to help you complete the challenge. So if you've got something you'd like to practice, feel free to give it a go.

  • View an age in years, months, and days after submitting a valid date through the form

assignments.title: Exercise 1: Printing even numbers
assignments.description_html:
Print all even numbers from 0 – 10

Try to make the solution to this problem as efficiently as possible. Consider using loops that would allow you to type the fewest characters/commands. While you could simply print the even numbers, get creative and see how you could output them in a way that would work up to 10 or even up to 10,000 with little extra effort.
